questions.question:
Consider the following program that attempts to locate an element \( x \) in an array \( a[ ] \) using binary search. Assume \( N > 1 \). The program is erroneous. Under what conditions does the program fail?

var i,j,k: integer; x: integer;
    a: array; [1..N] of integer;
begin	i:= 1; j:= n;
repeat	
    k:(i+j) div 2;
    if a[k] < x then i:= k
    else j:= k
until (a[k] = x) or (i >= j);
    
if (a[k] = x) then
    writeln ('x is in the array')
else
    writeln ('x is not in the array')
end;


GATE CSE 1996 | Question: 18

The average number of key comparisons required for a successful search for sequential search on \( n \) items is



GATE CSE 1996 | Question: 2.13, ISRO2016-28

Consider the following algorithm for searching for a given number \( x \) in an unsorted array \( A[1..n] \) having \( n \) distinct values:

  1. Choose an \( i \) at random from \( 1..n \)
  2. If \( A[i] = x \), then Stop else Goto 1;

Assuming that \( x \) is present in \( A \), what is the expected number of comparisons made by the algorithm before it terminates?



GATE CSE 2002 | Question: 2.10

Consider the following C program that attempts to locate an element \( x \) in an array \( Y[ \ ] \) using binary search. The program is erroneous. 

 f (int Y[10] , int x) {
    int i, j, k;
    i= 0; j = 9;
    do {
        k = (i+ j) / 2;
        if( Y[k] < x) i = k;else j = k;
        } while (Y[k] != x) && (i < j)) ;
    if(Y[k] == x) printf(" x is in the array ") ;
    else printf(" x is not in the array ") ;
 }

On which of the following contents of \( Y \) and \( x \) does the program fail? 



GATE CSE 2008 | Question: 84

Consider the following C program that attempts to locate an element \( x \) in an array \( Y[ \ ] \) using binary search. The program is erroneous. 

 f (int Y[10] , int x) {
    int i, j, k;
    i= 0; j = 9;
    do {
        k = (i + j) / 2;
        if( Y[k] < x) i = k;else j = k;
        } while (Y[k] != x) && (i < j)) ;
    if(Y[k] == x) printf(" x is in the array ") ;
    else printf(" x is not in the array ") ;
 }

The correction needed in the program to make it work properly is 



GATE CSE 2008 | Question: 85
options: Change line 6 to: if \( (Y[k] < x) i = k + 1 \); else \( j = k-1 \);
Change line 6 to: if \( (Y[k] < x) i = k - 1 \); else \( j = k +1 \);
Change line 6 to: if \( (Y[k] < x) i = k \); else \( j = k \);
questions.correct_answer: Change line 6 to: if \( (Y[k] < x) i = k + 1 \); else \( j = k-1 \);

questions.question:
Let \( A \) be an array of \( 31 \) numbers consisting of a sequence of \( 0 \)'s followed by a sequence of \( 1 \)'s. The problem is to find the smallest index \( i \) such that \( A\left [i \right ] \) is \( 1 \) by probing the minimum number of locations in \( A \). The worst case number of probes performed by an optimal algorithm is ____________.



GATE CSE 2017 Set 1 | Question: 48

​​​​Which of the following statements regarding Breadth First Search (BFS) and Depth First Search (DFS) on an undirected simple graph \( G \) is/are TRUE?



GATE CSE 2025 | Set 2 | Question: 19
options: A DFS tree of \( G \) is a Shortest Path tree of \( G \).
Every non-tree edge of \( G \) with respect to a DFS tree is a forward/back edge.

If \( (u, v) \) is a non-tree edge of \( G \) with respect to a BFS tree, then the distances from the source vertex \( s \) to \( u \) and \( v \) in the BFS tree are within \( \pm 1 \) of each other.
Both BFS and DFS can be used to find the connected components of \( G \).
correct_answer: Every non-tree edge of \( G \) with respect to a DFS tree is a forward/back edge.

If \( (u, v) \) is a non-tree edge of \( G \) with respect to a BFS tree, then the distances from the source vertex \( s \) to \( u \) and \( v \) in the BFS tree are within \( \pm 1 \) of each other.
Both BFS and DFS can be used to find the connected components of \( G \).
